text: this book is that the contemporary world has problematically incorporated liminality at its core. It has been proposed that the anthropological concept of liminality serves to throw critical light on a series of phenomena which are normally not analysed as part of the same reality. Such an analysis of the liminal characteristics can be pushed in many different directions. We have offered just some examples here. By way of conclusion it may be worth pointing towards other areas of contemporary life in which the concept of liminality shows its relevance as a diagnostic tool of the times in which we live. A proper substantiation of the claims made here must await future elaboration, but given the seriousness of the challenges we face, we risk these formulations. Globalization and Global Wars: The Loss of Boundaries Here one must quite obviously start with globalization. Cutting short the endless debates concerning a correct definition and theoretical explanation of the term, it is probably safe to say that globalization essentially has to do with the disappearance and breaking down of previously existing boundaries. This is visible already in the long series of terms used to pinpoint globalization as a process starting with a ‘de-’, from de-regulation to de-territorialization. Emblematically, the process of globalization was made possible by something that disappeared, a wall which was broken down and a boundary that was transgressed – and that from within the one city in Europe existing at the thresholds, in an institutionalized in-between position and geographic, political-economic limbo. For very good reasons, we celebrated this. But the optimism that characterized the early 1990s has slowly petered out; and in many ways some of the most problematic aspects of current globalization have to do with just such a continuous loss of limits. We have argued in Chapter 8 that revolutions – and to some extent the very vocabulary of political modernity – are quite evidently liminal at their core. At the level of politics, this relates to an understanding that also engages the liminal origins of democracy itself (Wydra 2009, Forlenza 2011). As these lines are being written, dramatic events in Syria and Egypt provide frightening evidence of what happens when the liminal period remains an open laboratory for self-declared By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 217 masters of ceremony and mass-produced mimetic violence; crisis is contagious, Turner said. These liminal features, alas, also involve the nature of contemporary international relations and global security politics. This analysis is currently being advanced by a handful of scholars working within international relations (see in particular Mälksoo 2012, 1 Yanik 2011). And there are indeed some extremely problematic ways in which the permanently liminal is at play also here. This became evident in the very naming of the ‘Global War on Terror’. Two essential features legitimized the claim to novelty here: that this war, in contrast to all previous ones, had no spatial or temporal boundaries. It is nowhere and everywhere, and it has no real end; it is boundless and essentially liminal. As Mälksoo states most explicitly, ‘Modern international conflict has turned the condition of perpetual liminality into a universal experience’ (2012: 492). The permanently liminal is visible also in the technologies of warfare. What could indicate this better than drones, these invisible robot-like 2 fighter planes hanging in the skies, ready to kill? We are told that this is our future ‘security paradigm’. One of the latest US drone programmes has been named the Gorgon Stare, evidently with reference to the Greek mythological figure whose gaze could turn victims into stone. These drone programs are not only killing people more efficiently. A primary goal of drone surveillance is to collect overhead imagery that will prove tactically useful. This quest for total knowledge amounts to global, total surveillance. This all forms part of what Wall and Monahan (2011) have aptly analysed as a permanently liminal, global security-space. This is not a question of taking sides; what America is doing now others will do tomorrow. And global terrorists are real enough. They are the contemporary exemplars of that figure identified by Marcel Mauss in his analysis of the Bolsheviks, namely the ‘professional revolutionary’. They may be organizing in novel types of networks and fleshing out their eschatological fantasies from a slightly different ideological core. Gnostic moderns they remain. And just like their predecessors, they expound an ideology which is purely abstract, based on empty slogans. Far from being faithful and self-effacing servants of the people, they are demagogues and adventurers, revelling in their return from exile, murky elements exploiting any opportunity to accumulate disorders and follies. They have no connection to and no genuine interest in the people they claim to fight for. While fancying themselves heirs to glorious revolutionary traditions, they are in fact mere imitators 1 The entire issue of Review of International Studies in which this article appeared was dedicated to a security-related discussion of liminality, with a foreword by Iver Neumann. 2 In robotics, great advances are currently made in producing killer machines, also operating from the ground and under water. It is worth remembering that the word robot was first introduced by the Czech writer Karel Čapek in his play Rossum’s Universal Robots, published in Prague in 1920. The science fiction play is about a factory producing artificial people. The robots seem happy to work for humans. Eventually this changes, and a hostile robot rebellion leads to the extinction of the human race. The play was first staged in 1921, and instantly made it from Central Europe to Broadway. Liminality and the Modern 218 of the ancient tyrants. They show no sign of care or commitment towards concrete, existential communities. Herein lies their globality and their permanent liminality. Liminal Economies It is generally agreed that our current, global world is mainly driven by the economy. The contemporary relevance for what is argued here would therefore need to indicate ways in which the implosion of liminal conditions is equally evident within the economic sphere. A central
